Late Antiquity

Pseudo-Dionysius

fl. c. 500 CE

The mystical theology that would shape a millennium of thought on what soul is and is not.

Here is the reconstruction that, in my judgment, does the most justice to Pseudo-Dionysius.

Framework

Pseudo-Dionysius does not write a separate, modern-style treatise “on the soul” or “on mind.” Still, the corpus has a coherent philosophical architecture, and it is reasonable to reconstruct a philosophy of mind from it. The Divine Names supplies the main ontology and epistemology of knowing, while The Celestial Hierarchy shows what created intelligence looks like across the angelic and human orders. That is why these two works are the right center of gravity for the question. Historically, the author is the late-fifth-/early-sixth-century Christian Neoplatonist conventionally called Pseudo-Dionysius, writing sometime after Proclus and before the first clear citations in the early sixth century.

A first caution is terminological. In Dionysius, nous is not merely “intellect” in a narrow, modern, analytic sense. One standard translation note observes that the term can extend toward spiritual subjectivity or “personality,” which helps explain why Dionysius can speak of angels as “minds.” At the same time, his vocabulary ranges across soul, reason, sense, imagination, faith, contact, and supra-intellectual union. So his psychology is not a neat faculty-chart in the later scholastic manner; it is a graded account of living, knowing, desiring, and being transformed.

The most basic methodological principle appears at the opening of The Divine Names. We may not say or think just anything about God. Divine things are given according to revelation and according to the capacity of each mind. That already means that mentality is, for Dionysius, intrinsically ordered, participatory, and unequal in degree: minds do not all know in the same way, nor are all capable of the same form of disclosure.

1. God is beyond mind, yet the source of all mind

Dionysius’s treatment of the divine case is indispensable, not incidental. God is called Mind, Reason, Wisdom, Light, Life, and so on, but never as one being among other beings, or one mind among other minds. God is “above” mind, reason, and wisdom. Even the noblest names fail if they are taken univocally. Thus the divine mind is not the top item in a common class; it is the superessential source from which every created mode of knowing derives.

Yet Dionysius does not reduce divine mind-language to a mere pious metaphor. In The Divine Names 7 he asks how God can be called Mind and Knowledge if God does not “think” by finite intellectual operations or know by sensible perception. His answer is that God knows all things causally, from himself and in himself, because he is their source. God does not learn from what already exists; he precontains and produces the reasons and being of things. In contemporary terms, Dionysius’s account of divine knowing is productive and causal rather than observational, inferential, or representational in the creaturely sense.

This is why the highest created relation to God is not ordinary concept-possession. Dionysius says the mind has a power of thought, but the union by which it touches what lies beyond it surpasses the mind’s own nature. Hence his language of agnosia or “unknowing”: not blank ignorance, but a supra-conceptual participation in what exceeds discursive and even intuitive grasp. The mind must, in his striking formulation, go out beyond its ordinary self-possession if it is to receive the divine.

That point matters for the whole philosophy of mind. For Dionysius, the highest mental act is not an especially clear representation of an object. It is a union in which the knower is transformed by what is known. Knowledge, at its summit, is participatory assimilation rather than detached inspection.

2. Angelic minds are the paradigm of created intellect

The angelic case is central because angels show what created intelligence is when it is relatively free from bodily discursivity. In The Divine Names 7, angelic minds receive simple and blessed conceptions, not in fragments, not from sense, not through step-by-step reasoning, but intuitively, immaterially, and uniformly. In The Celestial Hierarchy, the highest rank stands immediately around God and receives simple, immediate illuminations “without symbol.” This is Dionysius’s clearest image of created intellect in its highest mode.

Still, angelic intelligence is not homogeneous. It is hierarchically differentiated. The first hierarchy is purified, enlightened, and perfected directly by God; lower ranks receive illumination mediately, through higher ones, according to capacity. Dionysius therefore imagines created mind as intrinsically relational and pedagogical. Minds do not simply possess knowledge; they receive it, are shaped by it, and pass it on. Illumination circulates through ordered mediation.

The names of the angelic orders are themselves cognitive and ethical descriptions. Seraphim signify burning elevation and purifying fire; Cherubim fullness of knowledge and wisdom; Thrones stable receptivity to God; Lordships freedom from servility; Powers unflinching strength in divine operation; Authorities good order in reception and transmission. Dionysius does not separate intellect from moral formation or desire: an angelic “mind” is a way of knowing, willing, loving, and acting in right order.

Angelic cognition is also dynamic. Dionysius describes “divine minds” as moving circularly when united around the Good, directly when ministering to inferiors, and spirally in provident mediation. The angelic mind is not static contemplation alone; it is contemplative motion that receives, retains, and communicates illumination.

So if one asks what a created mind is for Dionysius, the angelic answer is: a luminous, immaterial, intuitive, hierarchical, and communicative intelligence whose perfection consists in likeness to God and care for what is below it.

3. The human soul is rational, discursive, embodied, and symbol-dependent

Human beings differ from angels not because they are irrational, but because their rationality is embodied and therefore indirect. Dionysius says quite plainly that our mind cannot be raised to immaterial contemplation without “material guidance” suited to it. Visible beauty, scent, light, ritual order, sacramental forms, and scriptural images are therefore not dispensable decorations. They are the ordinary vehicles by which the human mind is borne from multiplicity toward simple contemplation.

This is why symbolic theology matters so much. Dissimilar symbols can even be better than more obviously “noble” images, because they prevent the mind from collapsing heavenly reality into literal picture-thinking. The symbol is necessary, but it must also be transcended. Dionysius’s view of representation is therefore double: names and images are true as pedagogical mediations, and false if taken as exhaustive likenesses.

The human soul’s own proper mode is discursivity. Dionysius explicitly contrasts souls with angelic minds: souls investigate truth by divided, stepwise, rotating reasoning, whereas angels know intuitively and uniformly. Yet he does not leave the human soul trapped there. Human discursivity can be recollected, unified, and elevated. The human soul can become “equal to angels” in a qualified way, not by ceasing to be human, but by being gathered from multiplicity toward unity.

The famous passage in The Divine Names 9 on the three motions of soul is probably the most important single text for a Dionysian philosophy of mind. The soul moves circularly when it turns inward, gathers its powers, and unifies itself; spirally when it advances through discursive and relative reasoning under illumination; and straightly when it is led from external symbols to simple contemplation. That is a remarkably rich cognitive map: recollection, discursive mediation, and symbolic ascent are all legitimate and ordered modes of mentality.

Dionysius also gives a broader inventory of cognitive life than readers sometimes notice. God is said to be known and not known through conception, expression, science, contact, sensible perception, opinion, imagination, and name. Whatever exactly one does with each item, the point is unmistakable: his philosophy of mind is not restricted to bare intellection. It includes sensory, imaginative, linguistic, affective, and supra-conceptual modes, even while denying that any one of them can contain divine essence.

4. Embodiment is not a defect to be discarded

Dionysius is a strong non-materialist, but he is not a body-hating dualist in the crude sense. He explicitly denies that body is the cause of the soul’s baseness, and he denies that matter, simply as matter, is evil. Evil in bodies is privation of form and order, not the body as such. Matter participates in beauty and form; body can serve as symbol; and divine power “holds together the bond of soul and body.”

That is why bodily images can be used to disclose mental and angelic realities. In The Celestial Hierarchy 15, vision symbolizes receptive elevation to divine light; smell, discrimination; hearing, conscious reception of inspiration; taste, intelligible nourishment; touch, discernment of what suits or harms; eyelids and brows, the guarding of God-seeing conceptions; teeth, the division and distribution of unified understanding; heart, providential life; wings and feet, upward quickness and motion toward divine things. This is not physiology in the modern scientific sense. It is symbolic phenomenology: the body is read as a map of cognitive and spiritual functions.

So the human mind, for Dionysius, is not a pure intelligence accidentally burdened with sense. It is an embodied rational soul whose ordinary ascent requires sensory, imagistic, ritual, and linguistic mediation. Embodiment is not the last word, but it is a meaningful and providential stage in the soul’s mode of knowing.

5. Love, beauty, light, prayer, and faith belong to cognition itself

A decisive feature of Dionysius’s philosophy of mind is that he does not separate cognition from desire. In The Divine Names 4, the Good is also Beautiful and Love: it calls all things to itself, unifies them, and orders the rests and motions of minds, souls, and bodies. Love is not an optional emotional add-on to knowledge. It is the unifying power by which beings are moved toward their source and held together with one another.

That is why divine love is described as ecstatic. Love does not allow the lover to remain enclosed in itself. Dionysius applies this first to creatures—the superior caring for the inferior, equals cohering with equals, inferiors respecting superiors—and then, daringly, to God’s providential relation to all things. The point is not sentimental. It is metaphysical and psychological: the basic movement of mind is self-transcending attraction toward the Good.

Light functions similarly. Dionysius says spiritual light renews mental powers, expels ignorance, and gathers scattered and fanciful views into one pure and uniform knowledge. Illumination is therefore not just a metaphor for information transfer. It names the very process by which divided mentality becomes unified and truthful.

Prayer belongs inside this epistemology, not outside it. In The Divine Names 3, Dionysius says theology must begin with prayer, not because prayer drags God down into our vicinity, but because it repositions us and makes us present to the divine ray. Prayer is cognitive asceticism: it alters the subject so that the subject may receive what is already given.

Faith, too, is not merely an inferior substitute for knowledge. In The Divine Names 7, faith is the stable union of knower and truth that grants an “unmoved” identity, freeing one from wandering among manifold errors. In Dionysius, faith is epistemically serious: it is a mode of cognitive steadfastness and participation, not an arbitrary leap.

6. Error and evil are privation, weakness, and disordered motion

Dionysius’s account of mental failure is one of the most philosophically important parts of the picture. Evil is not a positive rival principle. In souls it is failure of good habits and energies, lack of strength, missing the mark, and disordered movement. In bodies it is being contrary to nature; in souls, contrary to reason; in demons, contrary to the good-like mind. So Dionysius’s moral psychology is at once ontological, epistemic, and teleological: to go wrong is to fall away from one’s proper mode of being and knowing.

The demon passage is especially revealing. Dionysius says that even the mind of demons, qua mind, comes from God. Their evil is not mind as such, but a decline from wisdom: irrationality, ignorance, and a refusal to attain what they desire. This means that for Dionysius mental nature remains good even in corruption; what is lost is right order, right movement, and right participation.

Mental pathology, then, is dispersion. Spiritual light collects; peace harmonizes; circular return recollects; hierarchy orders; love unifies. Error is the opposite pattern: scattering, privation, instability, and alienation from one’s proper good.

7. Mind is irreducibly social and hierarchical

The deepest formal category in Dionysius’s philosophy of mind is hierarchy. In The Celestial Hierarchy 3, hierarchy is defined as a sacred order, science, and operation whose purpose is assimilation and union with God. It perfects its members as luminous mirrors that receive primal light and pass it onward. This is not accidental imagery. It means that knowing is a circulation of illumination through ordered relations.

Accordingly, cognition is never merely private. The higher instruct the lower; the first hierarchy perfects the second and third; angels mediate to human beings; human hierarchs may even be called “angels” insofar as they participate in the revealing office. In contemporary vocabulary, one could say that Dionysius pictures mind as distributed across a community of mediation rather than locked inside isolated subjects. But the distribution is not horizontal or egalitarian. It is ordered by nearness to the source and by capacity for reception.

Importantly, union does not abolish distinction. Dionysius repeatedly says that gifts are given according to capacity, and that justice and preservation keep beings in their own proper order and good. Mystical union is therefore not an annihilation of rank, nature, or identity. It is perfected order: each being becomes most itself by becoming more transparent to the source from which it comes.

A modern scholar has suggested that Dionysius’s definition of hierarchy as epistēmē can be read alongside ecstasy: understanding arrives through an ordered “standing outside” oneself. Even if one takes that as an interpretive gloss rather than a strict lexical thesis, it captures something real in the texts. Dionysian knowing is self-transcendence under order, not autonomous mental self-enclosure.

What philosophy of mind emerges?

If one compresses all this into a single reconstruction, Dionysius offers a participatory, hierarchical, teleological, and non-reductionist philosophy of mind. Mind is not self-grounding; it exists by sharing in a source beyond mind. The central contrast is between angelic intuition and human discursivity, but human reason is not trapped in discursivity: it can be recollected, symbolically elevated, and united beyond itself. Sensation, imagination, language, prayer, faith, and love are not external to knowledge; they are stages or modalities within the human way of receiving truth. Error is not a positive substance but privative disorder and misdirected motion. And the highest form of knowing is not representation but communion.

Just as important is what Dionysius does not give. He gives no physiology of cognition, no neutral psychology abstracted from ethics and worship, and no account of mind as a sealed inner theater. His “philosophy of mind” is inseparable from metaphysics, liturgy, desire, and sanctification. That is not a defect in the reconstruction; it is the point. For Dionysius, to ask what mind is already means asking what it is for, how it is ordered, what heals or disorders it, and how it is raised into likeness with the Good.

So the most faithful summary is this: the soul/mind in Pseudo-Dionysius is a created power of reception, differentiation, recollection, and loving ascent. Angelic minds show the created possibility of intuitive and immaterial intelligence; human souls show how such intelligence is approached through embodied symbols, discursive purification, prayer, and faith; the divine “Mind” is the super-intellectual source whose knowing is creative and whose love ecstatically communicates being. The center of the whole account is not self-consciousness but deifying participation in the Beautiful and Good.

I can also turn this into a more formal scholarly outline keyed to chapter-and-section references in DN and CH, or into a thesis-driven comparison with Plotinus, Proclus, Augustine, or Maximus the Confessor.
